critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression design for equipment failure prediction in a manufacturing plant has a critical label construction problem that makes the entire comparison uninterpretable.

The 7-day positive label window means that any 168-hour window before a logged failure is labeled positive. The RNN processes a 168-hour lookback window to predict this 7-day label. The lookback window and the label window are therefore identical in length: the RNN is predicting 'will this machine fail in the next 7 days?' using the 'last 7 days of sensor data.' For the test set, this means the RNN sees 7 days of data to predict the next 7 days. The crucial question is whether the positive-labeled sequences (the 7-day windows before failures) are constructed so that the failure itself does not appear in the input window. If a failure occurs on day T and the positive label applies to the window [T-168h, T], but the sensor data for the input window also spans [T-168h, T], then the input window includes sensor readings from the hours immediately before and possibly including the failure event. If the sensors produce anomalous readings during the failure itself (vibration spike at T, temperature spike at T-1h), the model is being asked to predict failure using data that already shows the failure signature — this is a form of temporal leakage within the sequence window.

The logistic regression baseline uses the same 9 engineered features 'averaged or summarized over the 168-hour window.' Averaging kills the temporal ordering information that the RNN uses. If the failure signature is a late spike in vibration (high values in the last 24 hours of the window), the average over 168 hours dilutes this signal. The comparison therefore disadvantages the baseline not just through architectural capacity but through temporal information destruction. An appropriate comparison would give the logistic regression access to the last-24-hour averages as separate features from the earlier window averages.

F1 score is used as the primary optimization metric on the validation set. F1 depends on the classification threshold, which is typically set at 0.5 by default. In an imbalanced dataset (failures are rare), 0.5 is not the optimal threshold — the optimal threshold for maximizing F1 must be found via threshold sweep. The design does not specify that threshold optimization occurs during validation, and if the default threshold is used, both models are suboptimally calibrated and the F1 comparison is not a test of model quality but of threshold choice.

Stratification by machine type is used to ensure all machine types appear in train, validation, and test. But the design acknowledges that machines 'exhibit heterogeneous failure modes.' If a particular machine type has only 2-3 failure events in 36 months, each split may contain fewer than 1-2 failures for that type — the per-machine-type F1 scores in the secondary analysis will be computed on extremely small positive class counts and will be meaningless as performance estimates.

The SCADA sensor data at 'native sampling frequency' is not specified. If temperature, pressure, and vibration are sampled at 1-second intervals, the 168-hour window contains approximately 600,000 observations per machine — the rolling window features summarize these into 27 engineered features, but the choice of 1-hour and 24-hour rolling windows may miss failure precursors that occur at sub-hourly resolution (e.g., bearing failure precursors appear in vibration at frequencies of seconds to minutes).
defender_raw:
This experiment tests a Bi-LSTM against logistic regression for predictive maintenance failure prediction using sensor sequences. The design is methodologically appropriate for the industrial IoT setting.

The 168-hour (7-day) pre-failure label window is explicitly justified by the plant's maintenance scheduling cycle. This is the correct way to define the prediction horizon: it should match the actionable lead time the operations team needs, not an arbitrary window. Using a shorter window would not provide enough time for maintenance scheduling; a longer window would include normal operating periods, diluting the signal.

The 168-hour lookback window for the RNN sequence matches the label window. This is a sound design choice: the model's temporal context equals the predictive horizon, ensuring the sequence captures the full pre-failure signature that defines the positive class. Misaligning lookback and label windows would create a structural mismatch.

Stratification by machine type within the temporal split is appropriate. Different machines have different failure modes, sensor distributions, and base rates. Without stratification, rare machine types could be absent from the validation or test set, making the per-machine-type evaluation uninformative. Stratified splitting ensures fair per-type evaluation.

The logistic regression baseline uses the same 9 engineered features (averaged over the 168-hour window) as the RNN uses sequentially. This is the correct comparison: the baseline receives the same information in aggregated form, and the RNN receives it in sequential form. Any performance difference is attributable to the value of temporal ordering, not feature richness.

F1 score as primary metric is appropriate for the imbalanced failure prediction task. The plant manager's objective is balanced — false alarms waste maintenance labor, missed failures cause downtime. F1 directly captures this balance. The addition of Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) as a secondary metric is appropriate: MCC is more informative than F1 for severely imbalanced classes because it accounts for all four cells of the confusion matrix.
all_issues_raised: The positive label window (168 hours before failure) and the RNN input lookback window (168 hours) are identical in length — if the sensor data window includes hours immediately before the failure event where anomalous readings already appear, the RNN predicts failure from data that already contains the failure signature, constituting within-sequence temporal leakage.

The logistic regression baseline averages all features over the full 168-hour window, destroying the temporal ordering information that distinguishes a late failure spike from a gradual drift — the comparison disadvantages the baseline through information destruction, not just through architectural limitation.

F1 optimization on the validation set requires threshold calibration, but the design does not specify that threshold sweeping occurs — using the default 0.5 threshold in imbalanced failure-prediction settings will produce suboptimal F1 scores for both models and make the comparison a test of default threshold behavior rather than model quality.

Per-machine-type stratification may result in fewer than 2-3 failure events per type per split for rare machines — the secondary per-type F1 analysis will be computed on positive class counts too small to produce meaningful performance estimates.

The native SCADA sampling frequency is not specified, but if sensors are sampled at sub-minute intervals, the 1-hour rolling window features may miss high-frequency failure precursors (e.g., bearing wear signatures in vibration) that would be captured only with shorter window summaries.
all_issues_adjudicated: The positive label window (168 hours before failure) and the RNN input lookback window (168 hours) are identical in length — if the sensor data window includes hours immediately before the failure event where anomalous readings already appear, the RNN predicts failure from data that already contains the failure signature, constituting within-sequence temporal leakage.

The logistic regression baseline averages all features over the full 168-hour window, destroying the temporal ordering information that distinguishes a late failure spike from a gradual drift — the comparison disadvantages the baseline through information destruction, not just through architectural limitation.

F1 optimization on the validation set requires threshold calibration, but the design does not specify that threshold sweeping occurs — using the default 0.5 threshold in imbalanced failure-prediction settings will produce suboptimal F1 scores for both models and make the comparison a test of default threshold behavior rather than model quality.

Per-machine-type stratification may result in fewer than 2-3 failure events per type per split for rare machines — the secondary per-type F1 analysis will be computed on positive class counts too small to produce meaningful performance estimates.
